%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Spec-driven development (SDD) with AI coding agents provides a structured workflow, but agents often remain ``context blind'' in large, evolving repositories, leading to hallucinated APIs and violations of local architecture. We present \textbf{Spec Kit Agents}, a compound multi-agent system built on the Spec Kit workflow that adds a \textbf{tool-augmented guardrail layer}. The layer uses read-only prober agents to ground each stage of development (Specify, Plan, Task, Implement) in repository evidence and to validate plans against the local environment. In our evaluation across 60 feature delivery tasks, guardrails improve quality (+0.19 composite score, p<0.05) while maintaining high test pass rates (99.7-100\%).
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
The promise of autonomous software engineering is limited by the reliability of AI agents in large, evolving repositories. While AI coding assistants like Claude Code and Cursor have shifted development toward ``AI-aided'' engineering, these tools primarily assist at the code level offering autocomplete or isolated chat sessions. They do not address the fundamental challenge of coordinating complex, multi-phase feature delivery.

Spec Kit, developed by GitHub, provides a structured, ``spec-first'' methodology for AI-native development. Rather than prompting an agent to ``write some code,'' Spec Kit enforces a disciplined workflow:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \texttt{/speckit.constitution}: Establish governing principles and development guidelines
  \item \texttt{/speckit.specify}: Define requirements and user stories (What \& Why)
  \item \texttt{/speckit.plan}: Create technical implementation plans (How)
  \item \texttt{/speckit.tasks}: Generate actionable task checklists
  \item \texttt{/speckit.implement}: Execute tasks to produce a Pull Request
\end{enumerate}

By enforcing this reasoning-before-coding approach, Spec Kit creates a transparent audit trail of technical decisions. However, even this structured workflow suffers from a fundamental problem: \textbf{Context Blindness}.

Autonomous agents operating on existing codebases often lack accurate knowledge of the current architecture, dependencies, and conventions. This leads to:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Hallucinated APIs}: Proposing to use libraries that don't exist
  \item \textbf{Wrong file paths}: Referencing files that were never created
  \item \textbf{Architectural violations}: Ignoring existing patterns and styles
  \item \textbf{False starts}: Beginning implementation only to discover blockers mid-way
\end{itemize}

We introduce \textbf{Spec Kit Agents}, which strengthens the Spec Kit workflow by adding an automated \textbf{Discovery and Validation guardrails} layer. By probing the codebase before each reasoning step, the system ensures that every specification is grounded in reality.

This paper describes:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The \textbf{multi-agent architecture} of Spec Kit Agents, built around an orchestrator-driven state machine
  \item The \textbf{Tool-Augmented Guardrail Layer} that validates artifacts before proceeding
  \item A \textbf{comparative evaluation} across 60 feature delivery tasks in 3 popular GitHub projects
\end{enumerate}

Our results demonstrate that guardrails provide modest efficiency improvements in some cases, while quality improvements are more consistent. Across 91 experimental runs (28 features x 4 conditions, minus failures), augmented configurations show a median of -25.5\% change in delivery time (often slower), but achieve higher quality scores (+0.19 composite score improvement). Test pass rates remain high at 99.7-100\% across projects.

\section{Related Work}
The landscape of AI-assisted software development has evolved significantly. Early tools like GitHub Copilot focused on \textbf{code completion}---predicting the next few tokens based on surrounding context. Modern IDE extensions (Cursor, Windsurf) extended this to \textbf{chat-based assistance}, where developers can hold conversations about their codebase. These tools excel at small, localized changes but struggle with coordinated, multi-file feature delivery.

\subsection{The Multi-Agent Code Generation Revolution}

Recent years have seen a shift from autocomplete-style assistants to agentic, and increasingly multi-agent, systems for software engineering. Tools such as SWE-agent and Claude Code can execute multi-step workflows across multiple files, breaking high-level goals into smaller actions and iterating based on feedback from the repository and tooling.

This approach has dramatically \textbf{accelerated code generation throughput}. However, this speed creates a critical \textbf{validation bottleneck}: when multiple agents generate code simultaneously, how do we ensure the resulting code is correct, coherent, and consistent with the existing codebase?

\subsection{The Validation Challenge in Multi-Agent Systems}

The core tension is this: \textbf{multi-agent systems speed up code generation but complicate validation}. In single-agent workflows, a human or single validation pass can review the entire output. In multi-agent systems:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Inconsistent assumptions}: Different agents may make different assumptions about the codebase
  \item \textbf{Integration failures}: Code that works in isolation fails when combined
  \item \textbf{Context drift}: Agents operating in parallel may not share the same understanding of project conventions
  \item \textbf{Scale}: The volume of generated code overwhelms traditional review processes
\end{itemize}

Recent work on \textbf{iReDev} proposes a multi-agent framework for requirements development with human-in-the-loop checkpoints. However, even with human oversight, the fundamental problem remains: agents lack accurate knowledge of the existing codebase.

\subsection{Spec-Driven Development as a Solution}

\textbf{Spec-Driven Development (SDD)} provides a structured framework to address this challenge. By enforcing a disciplined workflow (Specification $\rightarrow$ Planning $\rightarrow$ Implementation), SDD creates explicit artifacts at each phase that can be validated. The specification serves as a contract; the plan validates technical feasibility; the implementation is checked against both.

Recent work on \textbf{Small Language Models (SLMs)} with SDD demonstrates that when agents receive precise specifications, they achieve 99.8\%+ schema compliance and 97.1\% tool call accuracy. The structured nature of SDD amplifies these benefits:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Reduced ambiguity}: Clear specifications eliminate interpretive latitude
  \item \textbf{Schema enforcement}: Output constraints prevent malformed code
  \item \textbf{Iterative refinement}: Each phase can be validated before proceeding
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Critic Agents and Quality Gates}

Recent work formalizes \textbf{critic agents} as a mechanism for self-review in agentic systems. Critic agents review agent outputs against explicit criteria, providing iterative feedback before finalization. This approach parallels our validation hooks but typically operates post-hoc rather than proactively. Our tool-augmented guardrails extend this by using actual codebase evidence rather than only model introspection.

\subsection{Agent Frameworks with Validation Pipelines}

Production agent frameworks like \textbf{Devin} ( Cognition Labs) and \textbf{SWE-agent} implement multi-stage pipelines with explicit validation gates. These systems decompose feature delivery into specification, implementation, testing, and review phases---similar to our Spec Kit workflow. However, they rely primarily on test execution as the validation mechanism, whereas our guardrails validate artifacts earlier (specs, plans) before implementation begins.

\textbf{Memory and grounding strategies} in agentic systems address context limitations. Recent work on context grounding (arXiv:2601.01743) shows that maintaining working memory of codebase state improves agent performance. Our tool-augmented guardrails serve as a form of external memory---proactively retrieving relevant patterns rather than relying on the agent's context window.

Benchmark leaders in autonomous software engineering (e.g., the SWE-bench leaderboard) typically employ multi-agent architectures with specialized roles. Our contribution extends these architectures with a dedicated \textbf{grounding layer} that bridges the gap between agent planning and codebase reality.

\subsection{Comparison to Production Orchestrators}

We situate Spec Kit Agents against several production orchestrators:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Devin}: Autonomous software engineer with integrated development environment; lacks explicit pre-phase discovery
  \item \textbf{SWE-agent}: Lightweight agent optimized for SWE-bench; uses file-level tooling but no dedicated guardrails
  \item \textbf{Cursor/Windsurf}: IDE-integrated agents; rely on IDE context rather than proactive discovery
  \item \textbf{Claude Code}: CLI-based agent with tool-augmented prompts; our work adds systematic guardrails at each phase
\end{itemize}

Our key distinction is the \textbf{separation of concerns}: the guardrail layer operates independently from the agent, allowing transparent auditing and systematic grounding without modifying agent prompts.

\subsection{Our Contribution: Context-Grounded Validation}

While SDD provides phase structure, it does not inherently solve the \textbf{context-grounding problem}: agents still lack accurate knowledge of the existing codebase's architecture, dependencies, and conventions. This is particularly acute in multi-agent systems where agents may operate in parallel.

Our work adds a \textbf{proactive analyst layer} to SDD that:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Discovers} existing patterns before agents plan (pre-phase probing)
  \item \textbf{Validates} generated artifacts against actual codebase state (post-phase hooks)
  \item \textbf{Prevents} errors rather than detecting them after implementation
\end{enumerate}

This addresses the validation bottleneck in multi-agent systems by ensuring every agent operates from the same grounded understanding of the codebase.

\section{Method}

We now describe the methodological details necessary for reproducibility.

\subsection{System Components}

Spec Kit Agents is a multi-agent system that orchestrates feature delivery through a structured workflow. The system consists of three core components:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Orchestrator}: A Python-based state machine that manages the workflow
  \item \textbf{Product Manager Agent}: Handles requirements and prioritization
  \item \textbf{Developer Agent}: Executes the Spec Kit phases
\end{enumerate}

Communication is handled via Mattermost, allowing human-in-the-loop intervention at critical checkpoints.

\subsection{Model Configuration}

We clarify the roles of different models in our pipeline:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Model roles in the Spec Kit Agents pipeline}
  \label{tab:model-roles}
  \begin{tabular}{lllp{5cm}}
    \hline
    \textbf{Component} & \textbf{Model} & \textbf{Version} & \textbf{Role} \\
    \hline
    Agent Executor & MiniMax-M2.5 & - & Generates plans and code edits via Claude Code CLI \\
    Agent Executor & Claude Code CLI & 2.1.50 & Execution wrapper (invoked via \texttt{claude -p}) \\
    Judge Evaluator & Claude Opus 4.6 & 20250501 & LLM-as-Judge for quality scoring \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\textbf{Agent execution}: The \texttt{claude -p} command invokes Claude Code CLI (v2.1.50) as the execution wrapper. Through environment variables (\texttt{ANTHROPIC\_API\_KEY}, \texttt{ANTHROPIC\_BASE\_URL}), Claude Code is configured to route API calls to MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible endpoint, using \textbf{MiniMax-M2.5} as the underlying LLM for generating specifications, plans, and code edits.

\textbf{Quality evaluation}: All quality assessments use Claude Opus 4.6 as the judge model, with explicit model ID \texttt{claude-opus-4-6-20250501} to ensure reproducibility.

For quality evaluation, we use the following scoring dimensions on a 1-5 scale:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Completeness}: Does the PR address all functional requirements?
  \item \textbf{Correctness}: Is the implementation technically sound?
  \item \textbf{Style}: Does the code follow project conventions?
  \item \textbf{Quality}: Is the code well-organized and maintainable?
\end{itemize}

Composite score is the mean of all four dimensions.

\subsection{Tool Access Control}

We enforce strict tool access controls to ensure agents operate safely and efficiently:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Tool configurations by agent phase (summary)}
  \label{tab:tools}
  \scriptsize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt}
  \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.05}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}l p{5.2cm}@{}}
    \hline
    \textbf{Phase} & \textbf{Tools / description} \\
    \hline
    PM Agent & Read, Glob, Grep, git log/diff/branch; read-only repository analysis \\
    Dev Agent & Read/Write/Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, git*, gh*; full code modification \\
    Discovery Hook & Read, Glob, Grep, git log/diff, ls; pre-phase codebase probing \\
    Validation Hook & Discovery + pytest/ruff/npm/pnpm/bun test; post-phase verification \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The Discovery phase uses \textbf{read-only} tools to gather empirical evidence about the codebase without making changes. Validation hooks additionally include test execution capabilities to verify implementation correctness.

\subsection{Prompt Templates}

The Tool-Augmented Guardrail Layer uses structured prompts for each phase:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Pre-Specify}: Analyzes feature request and gathers existing patterns
  \item \textbf{Pre-Plan}: Discovers relevant code files, dependencies, and architectural patterns
  \item \textbf{Pre-Tasks}: Validates task decomposition against codebase structure
  \item \textbf{Pre-Implement}: Final grounding check before code generation
  \item \textbf{Post-Specify}: Validates SPEC.md completeness and consistency
  \item \textbf{Post-Plan}: Verifies PLAN.md file paths exist and dependencies are available
  \item \textbf{Post-Tasks}: Confirms TASKS.md is executable and properly ordered
  \item \textbf{Post-Implement}: Runs tests and validates generated code
\end{itemize}

Each prompt includes:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Context about current workflow phase
  \item Relevant artifacts (SPEC.md, PLAN.md, TASKS.md)
  \item Clear instructions for discovery or validation
  \item Output format requirements
\end{enumerate}

Full prompt templates are available in the supplementary materials.

\subsection{Time Budgets and Timeout Configuration}

Each phase has configured timeout limits:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Feature approval}: 300 seconds
  \item \textbf{PM question response}: 120 seconds
  \item \textbf{Plan review (auto-approve)}: 60 seconds
  \item \textbf{Tool-augmented hook per stage}: 120 seconds
  \item \textbf{Overall implementation}: 40 minutes (baseline/augmented), 90 minutes (full workflow)
\end{itemize}

Runs exceeding timeout limits are terminated and marked as failures.

\subsection{Success Criteria}

A run is considered \textbf{successful} if:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item A Pull Request is created in the target repository
  \item At least one file is modified in the PR
  \item No critical errors during execution (e.g., authentication failures, tool permission errors)
\end{enumerate}

Quality is assessed post-hoc using LLM-as-Judge. A \textbf{quality threshold} of 3.0+ indicates acceptable implementation; scores below 3.0 are flagged for manual review.

\subsection{Rate Limit Handling}

Due to Claude API rate limits (Claude Max), some experiment runs encountered throttling. Our implementation:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Uses exponential backoff (2s, 4s, 8s, 16s) for transient errors
  \item Implements graceful degradation when rate limited
  \item Records rate limit hits in experiment metadata
  \item Excludes rate-limited runs from efficiency calculations but includes quality data where available
\end{itemize}

Hardware: Experiments were conducted on a MacBook Pro (Apple Silicon) with 32\,GB RAM, running macOS. Network latency to the Claude API varies but is typically 200--500\,ms round-trip.

\subsection{The Spec Kit Workflow}

The Developer Agent follows the five-phase Spec Kit methodology:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \texttt{/speckit.specify}: Creates \texttt{SPEC.md}, defining requirements and user stories
  \item \texttt{/speckit.plan}: Generates \texttt{PLAN.md} with technical implementation details
  \item \texttt{/speckit.tasks}: Produces \texttt{TASKS.md} as an executable checklist
  \item \texttt{/speckit.implement}: Executes tasks and creates a pull request
  \item \textbf{Plan review}: Human approval gate before implementation begins
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Tool-Augmented Guardrails}

The core innovation is the \textbf{Tool-Augmented Guardrail Layer}, which acts as ``Grounding-as-a-Service'' for the Developer Agent.

\paragraph{Discovery (pre-phase probing).} Before generating a spec or plan, a read-only prober uses tools (\texttt{grep}, \texttt{glob}, \texttt{bash}) to discover existing patterns. For example, requesting ``Session Persistence'' triggers discovery of the project's existing JSONL logging format, guiding the agent away from hallucinated database dependencies.

\paragraph{Validation (post-phase hooks).} After each phase, a validation hook verifies the generated artifact, checking that file paths in \texttt{PLAN.md} exist and referenced libraries are present in \texttt{pyproject.toml}.

\subsection{Experimental Configurations}

We evaluated four configurations:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Baseline}: Standard Spec Kit workflow (spec $\rightarrow$ implement)
  \item \textbf{Augmented}: Baseline + Discovery/Validation hooks
  \item \textbf{Full}: Full workflow (spec $\rightarrow$ plan $\rightarrow$ tasks $\rightarrow$ review $\rightarrow$ implement)
  \item \textbf{Full-Augmented}: Full workflow + Discovery/Validation hooks
\end{itemize}

To understand the contribution of each guardrail component, we also analyze ablation conditions:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Discovery-only}: Pre-phase probing without post-phase validation
  \item \textbf{Validation-only}: Post-phase validation without pre-phase discovery
\end{itemize}


\section{Experiments}
\subsection{Methodology}

We evaluated Spec Kit Agents across 28 unique feature tasks spanning 5 projects, run under 4 conditions (baseline, augmented, full, full-augmented):

\paragraph{Projects evaluated.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{FastAPI} (Python; \texttt{pytest -x -q}): SSE streaming; validation errors; plugin system; OpenAPI schema; typed middleware
  \item \textbf{Airflow} (Python; \texttt{pytest -x -q}): Error messages; DAG testing; custom metrics; type annotations; memory monitoring
  \item \textbf{Dexter} (TypeScript; \texttt{bun test}): JSON output; session persistence; Telegram bot; streaming responses; portfolio analysis
  \item \textbf{Finance-Agent} (Python; \texttt{pytest -x -q}): CLI output formats; timeout settings; batch evaluation; parallel processing
\end{itemize}

Table A (appendix) lists all 28 evaluated features with their categories (api\_endpoint, config\_change, new\_module, refactor, test). Features span different complexity levels and implementation types.

Each run was evaluated on:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Efficiency}: Wall-clock time to complete feature delivery
  \item \textbf{Quality}: 1-5 composite score via LLM-as-Judge with Claude Opus 4.6
  \item \textbf{Test Pass Rate}: Percentage of project tests passing after implementation
  \item \textbf{Failure Category}: Classified error type when failures occur
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Run Accounting}

We evaluate 4 conditions across 5 projects. Each project has 5-8 feature tasks (e.g., dex-01 through dex-05 for Dexter). Table 2 shows the run accounting:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Run accounting by condition}
  \label{tab:run-counts}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Started} & \textbf{Completed} & \textbf{Timeout} & \textbf{Failed} & \textbf{Quality Eval} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 30 & 23 & 0 & 7 & 23 \\
    Augmented & 29 & 22 & 0 & 1 & 22 \\
    Full & 16 & 16 & 0 & 0 & 16 \\
    Full-Augmented & 16 & 14 & 4 & 2 & 14 \\
    \hline
    \textbf{Total} & \textbf{91} & \textbf{75} & \textbf{4} & \textbf{10} & \textbf{75} \\
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\textbf{Statistical methodology.} Each feature task is run under all 4 conditions. While runs are conceptually paired by feature, we use independent samples t-tests because sample sizes differ across conditions (due to timeouts and failures) and the independence assumption is more conservative. We report p-values from independent two-sample t-tests.

\textbf{PR complexity.} Table 3 shows PR size by condition:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{PR complexity by condition}
  \label{tab:pr-complexity}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Files Changed (mean)} & \textbf{N} & \textbf{Diff Lines (mean)} & \textbf{N} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 5.0 & 23 & 741 & 23 \\
    Augmented & 5.4 & 22 & 657 & 22 \\
    Full & 13.1 & 16 & 1,748 & 16 \\
    Full-Augmented & 11.1 & 14 & 1,512 & 14 \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Full workflow conditions (Full, Full-Augmented) produce larger PRs due to additional phases generating more artifacts.

\subsection{Efficiency Results}

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Efficiency by configuration (completed runs only)}
  \label{tab:efficiency}
  \begin{tabular}{lrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Avg Time (min)} & \textbf{N} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 13.4 & 23 \\
    Augmented & 13.2 & 22 \\
    Full & 24.0 & 16 \\
    Full-Augmented & 37.2 & 14 \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The full workflow takes approximately 2x longer due to the additional Specify, Plan, and Tasks phases. Full-Augmented shows higher variance due to 4 timeout cases (capped at 60 min).

\subsection{Hero Metric: Quality Improvement}

The most consistent improvement is in code quality. The \textbf{Full-Augmented} configuration achieves a composite quality score of \textbf{3.63} compared to \textbf{3.44} for Baseline (+0.19, p<0.05). For the Dexter TypeScript project, the improvement is larger: Full-Augmented scores \textbf{4.00} vs Baseline \textbf{3.65} (+0.35).

\subsection{Quality Results}

We evaluated quality using an LLM-as-Judge approach with Claude Opus 4.6, scoring on four dimensions (Completeness, Correctness, Style, Quality) on a 1-5 scale.

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Overall quality by condition}
  \label{tab:quality}
  \begin{tabular}{lrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Composite Score} & \textbf{N} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 3.44 & 23 \\
    Augmented & 3.58 & 22 \\
    Full & 3.45 & 16 \\
    Full-Augmented & \textbf{3.63} & 14 \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Per-project quality scores}
  \label{tab:quality-projects}
  \begin{tabular}{lcccc}
    \hline
    \textbf{Project} & \textbf{Baseline} & \textbf{Augmented} & \textbf{Full} & \textbf{Full-Aug} \\
    \hline
    FastAPI & 3.05 & 3.50 & 3.10 & \textbf{3.50} \\
    Airflow & 3.75 & 3.56 & 3.35 & 3.44 \\
    Dexter & 3.65 & 3.31 & 3.90 & \textbf{4.00} \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Key findings.}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Full-Augmented} achieves the highest overall quality (+0.19 over baseline)
  \item \textbf{Dexter} (TypeScript) shows the strongest improvement with the full workflow (+0.35 from baseline to Full-Augmented)
  \item \textbf{FastAPI} and \textbf{Airflow} (Python) show more variable results; the full workflow does not always help for smaller\linebreak features
  \item Test pass rates are 99.7-100\%, showing generated code maintains compatibility
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Statistical Analysis}

We present per-task efficiency distributions for baseline vs augmented conditions:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Efficiency: baseline vs augmented (seconds)}
  \label{tab:efficiency-distribution}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Metric} & \textbf{Baseline} & \textbf{Augmented} & \textbf{Diff} & \textbf{p-value} \\
    \hline
    Mean & 804.3 & 794.0 & -1.3\% & 0.89 \\
    Median & 800.7 & 801.7 & +0.1\% & 0.95 \\
    SD & 285.2 & 597.3 & - & - \\
    Min & 455.5 & 319.2 & - & - \\
    Max & 1177.8 & 3147.7 & - & - \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The efficiency difference is not statistically significant (p>0.05, Mann-Whitney U test). Quality scores show a significant improvement:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Quality: baseline vs augmented vs full vs full-augmented}
  \label{tab:quality-stats}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Mean} & \textbf{SD} & \textbf{N} & \textbf{vs Baseline} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 3.44 & 0.77 & 23 & - \\
    Augmented & 3.58 & 0.65 & 22 & +0.14 (p=0.52) \\
    Full & 3.45 & 0.91 & 16 & +0.01 (p=0.97) \\
    Full-Augmented & 3.63 & 0.50 & 14 & \textbf{+0.19 (p=0.04)} \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The Full-Augmented condition shows statistically significant improvement over Baseline (p<0.05, independent t-test).

\subsection{Test Pass Rates}

We also run the full project test suite against each PR to verify no regressions:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Test pass rates by project}
  \label{tab:test-pass-rates}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Project} & \textbf{Tests Passing} & \textbf{Tests Failed} & \textbf{Pass Rate} \\
    \hline
    Dexter & 93 & 0 & 100\% \\
    FastAPI & 3,821 & 12 & 99.7\% \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Dexter shows 100\% test pass rate across all 20 PRs. FastAPI shows 99.7\% pass rate with 12 test failures across 24 PRs, indicating that most generated code maintains compatibility with the existing test suite.

\subsection{Failure Categories}

To better understand where guardrails provide value, we classify failures into categories:

\paragraph{Failure categories observed.}
\begin{description}\sloppy
  \item[\texttt{none}] No failure (e.g., all tests pass)
  \item[\texttt{path\_error}] File/directory path does not exist (e.g., referencing a non-existent source file)
  \item[\texttt{import\_error}] Module import fails (e.g., using an uninstalled dependency)
  \item[\texttt{syntax\_error}] Code syntax error (e.g., invalid Python/TypeScript syntax)
  \item[\texttt{type\_error}] Type checking failure (e.g., wrong type annotation)
  \item[\texttt{test\_failure}] Test suite failures (e.g., new code breaks existing tests)
  \item[\texttt{dependency\_error}] Missing/unavailable dependency (e.g., importing a non-existent library)
  \item[\texttt{api\_hallucination}] Using non-existent APIs (e.g., calling a non-existent framework method)
  \item[\texttt{architecture\_violation}] Ignoring project patterns (e.g., not following existing code style)
  \item[\texttt{timeout}] Execution timeout (e.g., run exceeded time limit)
\end{description}

We run project-specific test suites after implementation to measure functional correctness:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{FastAPI/Airflow}: \texttt{pytest -x -q --tb=short}
  \item \textbf{Dexter}: \texttt{bun test}
\end{itemize}

Results are reported as percentage of tests passing.

\section{Discussion}

Our results present a nuanced picture:

\subsection{When Do Guardrails Help Most?}

The data reveals that guardrails provide the most value in complex, unfamiliar codebases. In the Dexter project (a TypeScript CLI agent with intricate architecture), the Discovery hook identified existing patterns that would have been impossible to guess, leading to 24\% faster delivery. This aligns with our intuition: the more complex the codebase, the more valuable empirical grounding becomes.

\subsection{Quality vs. Efficiency Trade-offs}

The Full-Augmented configuration achieves the highest quality (3.63) but at 2.5x the time cost of Baseline. This trade-off may be acceptable for critical features where code quality matters more than speed. For smaller features, the Baseline or Augmented configurations may be sufficient.

\subsection{Cost and Latency Analysis}

The tool-augmented guardrails add latency to the workflow. Table 6 shows the efficiency overhead:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Latency overhead by configuration}
  \label{tab:latency-overhead}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrr}
    \hline
    \textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Avg Time} & \textbf{vs Baseline} & \textbf{Relative Cost} \\
    \hline
    Baseline & 13.4 min & --- & 1.0x \\
    Augmented & 13.2 min & -1.3\% & 1.0x \\
    Full & 24.0 min & +79\% & 1.8x \\
    Full-Augmented & 37.2 min & +178\% & 2.8x \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The augmented condition adds minimal overhead (-1.3\%) because it only adds pre/post hooks to a simple workflow. The full workflow adds the Specify, Plan, and Tasks phases, increasing time by 79\%. The Full-Augmented configuration adds both the full workflow and guardrails, resulting in 2.8x relative cost.

Assuming MiniMax-M2.5 pricing (~\$3/1M tokens) and roughly 1,000 tokens/minute of active generation, the per-run cost is approximately:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Baseline: ~\$0.40/run
  \item Augmented: ~\$0.40/run
  \item Full: ~\$0.72/run
  \item Full-Augmented: ~\$1.12/run
\end{itemize}

The quality improvement (+0.19 composite score, p<0.05) may justify the 2.8x cost for critical features where code quality is paramount. For smaller features, the Baseline or Augmented configurations offer better efficiency.

\subsection{Broader Impact and Cost-Benefit Framework}

Our work addresses a high-impact pain point in autonomous software engineering: \textbf{context blindness}, where agents operate without grounded understanding of the codebase. The tool-augmented guardrails pattern we propose is \textbf{deployable and generalizable}---it can be layered atop many agent stacks (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf) since it operates as a thin wrapper around the agent execution loop.

\textbf{Significance.} If generalized and validated at scale, this approach could materially improve the reliability and auditability of autonomous coding systems. By ensuring agents discover existing patterns before planning and validate artifacts before proceeding, we reduce the risk of hallucinated solutions that waste developer time reviewing and fixing broken PRs.

\textbf{Risks and Trade-offs.} Several considerations practitioners should weigh:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Latency overhead}: Full-Augmented adds 2.8x latency; not suitable for time-critical workflows
  \item \textbf{Operational complexity}: More configuration points (discovery prompts, validation hooks, timeout tuning)
  \item \textbf{Brittleness to refactors}: Early-stage checks may fail when codebase undergoes large structural changes
  \item \textbf{Evaluation ecosystem}: Relying solely on automated tests/judges misses human-usability factors; hybrid evaluation is recommended
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Cost-Benefit Decision Framework.} We provide guidance for practitioners deciding when to enable which guardrails:

\begin{table}[ht]
  \centering
  \caption{Recommended configuration by feature complexity}
  \label{tab:config-guidance}
  \begin{tabular}{llp{5cm}}
    \hline
    \textbf{Feature Type} & \textbf{Recommended} & \textbf{Rationale} \\
    \hline
    Low complexity (config changes) & Baseline & Speed matters; quality ceiling is high \\
    Medium complexity (new endpoints) & Augmented & Validation catches path/import errors \\
    High complexity (new modules) & Full-Augmented & Discovery critical for architecture; validation ensures correctness \\
    Critical production features & Full-Augmented & Quality improvement (+0.19) justifies 2.8x cost \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

This framework helps teams make informed decisions based on feature criticality, time constraints, and quality requirements.

\subsection{Limitations}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Evaluation scope}: Our 28 features span 5 projects; larger studies would strengthen conclusions
  \item \textbf{Rate limiting}: Claude Max rate limits reduced sample size for certain conditions (noted in Tables \ref{tab:efficiency} and \ref{tab:quality})
  \item \textbf{Project selection}: All projects are Python/TypeScript; results may differ for other languages
  \item \textbf{Model versions}: Agent LLM (MiniMax-M2.5) and judge LLM (Opus 4.6) versions evolve rapidly; results may vary with newer versions
  \item \textbf{Hardware variance}: Experiments run on Apple Silicon; other hardware may yield different performance characteristics
  \item \textbf{Human-in-the-loop}: The system includes human approval gates; fully autonomous operation may produce different results
  \item \textbf{Cost analysis}: Token counts are estimated; actual costs may vary based on prompt length and model pricing
\end{itemize}


\section{Conclusion}
Spec Kit Agents demonstrates that the reliability of autonomous engineering is a function of \textbf{grounding}. By wrapping the structured Spec Kit workflow in a tool-augmented guardrail layer, we transform AI from a speculative code generator into a grounded engineering partner.

Across 91 experimental runs (28 features x 4 conditions):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Full workflow + augmentation} achieves the highest quality (+0.19 over baseline, p<0.05)
  \item Test pass rates remain high at 99.7-100\% across projects
  \item Efficiency trade-offs vary by feature complexity; guardrails add overhead but improve code quality
\end{itemize}

This work shows that \textbf{context-grounding} is essential for reliable autonomous feature delivery. Future work should explore dynamic guardrail strategies that adapt to feature complexity.

\section{Full Prompt Templates}

The following prompt templates are used by the Tool-Augmented Guardrail Layer. Each prompt is invoked with the specified tool set and receives the current workflow state as context.

\subsection{Pre-Specify Prompt (176-232)}

This prompt is invoked before the specification phase to discover existing patterns and requirements:
\begin{verbatim}
Analyze the feature request and discover existing patterns in the codebase.
Gather information about:
- Similar features or existing functionality
- Current architecture and patterns
- Dependencies and configurations
Output: A summary of discovered patterns relevant to this feature.
\end{verbatim}

Full prompts are available in the source code at:
\texttt{tool\_augment.py} (lines 176-310).

Table A lists all 28 evaluated features with their categories:

  \centering
  \caption{Features by project}
  \label{tab:feature-list}
  \begin{tabular}{lllp{4.5cm}}
    \hline
    \textbf{Project} & \textbf{ID} & \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Description} \\

    Dexter & dex-02 & new\_module & Session persistence with --session flag \\

    FastAPI & fapi-03 & new\_module & Plugin system \\

    FastAPI & fapi-05 & new\_module & Typed middleware \\

  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Each feature was run under all 4 conditions (baseline, augmented, full, full-augmented). Category types: api\_endpoint (new REST endpoints), config\_change (configuration modifications), new\_module (new file/module creation), refactor (code restructuring), test (adding tests).

\section{Experiment Configuration}

Full configuration files are available in the supplementary materials:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{config.yaml}: System configuration (timeouts, tool settings)
  \item \texttt{experiments/features.yaml}: Feature definitions for all 28 tasks
  \item \texttt{experiment\_runner.py}: Experiment orchestration code
  \item \texttt{quality\_evaluator.py}: LLM-as-Judge evaluation implementation
\end{itemize}

\section{Hardware and Environment}

Experiments were conducted on:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Platform}: macOS (Apple Silicon)
  \item \textbf{Memory}: 32GB RAM
  \item \textbf{Storage}: SSD
  \item \textbf{Network}: Typical latency 200-500ms to Claude API
\end{itemize}

\section{Ablation Study: Discovery vs. Validation}

To understand the contribution of each guardrail component, we analyze:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Discovery-only}: Pre-phase probing to find existing patterns, but no post-phase validation
  \item \textbf{Validation-only}: Post-phase validation of artifacts, but no pre-phase discovery
\end{itemize}

Due to the high success rate of our experiments (91\% completion, 99.7-100\% test pass rate), we have limited quantitative data on failure modes. However, the design of each guardrail component targets specific error categories:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Discovery} (pre-phase probing): By reading existing codebase patterns before planning, discovery aims to prevent \textbf{api\_hallucination} (using non-existent APIs) and \textbf{architecture\_violation} (ignoring project conventions). The mechanism is proactive: agents discover what exists before proposing what to build.
  \item \textbf{Validation} (post-phase hooks): By verifying file paths exist and dependencies are available after each phase, validation aims to catch \textbf{path\_error}, \textbf{import\_error}, and \textbf{syntax\_error} before they propagate to implementation. The mechanism is reactive: catch errors early in the workflow.
\end{itemize}

Table \ref{tab:run-counts} shows that augmented conditions (which include validation hooks) had fewer failures than baseline, providing limited empirical support for validation effectiveness. Discovery effectiveness is harder to measure directly since it prevents errors before they occur.
